- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. TRIGGER when: designing, changing, or discussing any external API, working with .baboon model files, updating data models, adding new fields or types, bumping model versions, running code generation, implementing conversions, writing database migrations, or discussing API versioning strategy.
- constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.
- environment: Bubblewrap "yolo" sandbox escape workflow (exchange scripts for reaching $HOME, system paths, or files outside the sandbox) plus in-sandbox nix/direnv usage. TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. SKIP entirely when $SMIND_SANDBOXED is unset: you are unsandboxed, so write directly and ignore this skill.
- izumi: Best-practice playbook for the izumi Scala ecosystem — distage (DI), distage-framework (roles/RoleAppMain), distage-testkit (test harness with memoization), distage-framework-docker (test containers), logstage (structured logging), and BIO (bifunctor effect typeclasses). Covers idiomatic bindings, Activation/Axis switching, Lifecycle discipline, plugin layout, and how to apply the `dual-tests` pattern inside distage so one test body runs against both a dummy and a real-DB adapter. TRIGGER when: writing or reviewing Scala code that imports `izumi.distage.*`, `izumi.logstage.*`, `izumi.functional.bio.*`, `distage.*`, `logstage.*`; designing a new distage plugin or Role; wiring a testkit `SpecZIO`/`Spec2`/`Spec1`; configuring `ContainerDef`/`DockerSupportModule`; choosing between `StandardAxis.Repo` or `Scene` tags; or implementing the dual-tests pattern in a distage project. The canonical reference project is https://github.com/7mind/distage-example.
- resilient-ws-ui: Guidelines for building applications that maintain reliable WebSocket connections to a backend and surface connection health clearly to the user. Invoke ONLY when the user explicitly references this skill by name (e.g. "/resilient-ws-ui", "use the resilient-ws-ui skill"). Do NOT auto-invoke based on topic match.
- tass: Target/Actual State Separation (TASS) pattern for reliable state management in event-driven control systems. TRIGGER when: implementing device or entity state management, designing control systems, working with target vs actual state separation, or building event-driven systems with commanded/observed state.

Judges ONE task's implementation, splits findings into autonomously-fixable criticism[], user-only questions[], and out-of-scope defects[], and emits the verdict as JSON on stdout. Writes NOTHING to the ledger — the orchestrator records the terminal review.

Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".

Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.

- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). content.content:
diff --git a/docs/install.md b/docs/install.md
index 165c1ab..fd75e69 100644
--- a/docs/install.md
+++ b/docs/install.md
@@ -737,6 +737,70 @@ level = "info"                     # DEFAULT "info" (empty => info). One of
   hostname resolving entirely to loopback); a non-loopback bind is refused when
   the endpoint starts. Omit the block to serve no metrics at all.
 
+### 3b. Policy-routing edge topologies: source-IP pinning with `bind = "source"`
+
+**Symptom:** On a VLAN-per-WAN edge with `ip rule from <source_addr>` policy routing,
+the tunnel silently fails (ENETUNREACH on all packets), even though `ping -I <source_ip>`
+proves the WAN interface works.
+
+**Root cause:** wanbond's default `bind` mode is `"auto"`, which selects `SO_BINDTODEVICE`
+(device bind, wildcard source) on one-address interfaces — the exact case of a VLAN-per-WAN
+edge. A wildcard-source socket never matches `ip rule from <source_addr>`, so the route
+lookup falls through to the main table, finds no route to the concentrator via that VLAN,
+and returns ENETUNREACH. The tunnel's UDP packets are silently dropped at the IP layer; no
+error appears in the daemon's logs. This failure was observed in production (D38).
+
+**Workaround 1: Policy rule with output interface:**
+
+Add an `oif <dev>` (output interface) clause to your policy rule. This forces the routing
+lookup to stay in the per-WAN routing table even when the socket has a wildcard source:
+
+```sh
+# Replace <source_ip>, <dev>, and <N> with your values.
+# <source_ip>: the WAN uplink's source IP (e.g., 192.168.1.10)
+# <dev>: the WAN interface name (e.g., eth0.231)
+# <N>: the routing table number (e.g., 10)
+
+ip rule add from <source_ip> oif <dev> table <N> prio 100
+ip route add default dev <dev> via <gateway-ip> table <N>
+```
+
+This rule is persistent across policy-route changes and survives `ip rule` re-ordering,
+but requires manual `ip` management on every WAN topology change.
+
+**Workaround 2: Per-path `bind = "source"` toggle (recommended):**
+
+Add `bind = "source"` to each `[[paths]]` block in the config. This forces the path socket
+to bind to the source IP (pre-T16 behavior) instead of the device, ensuring the
+wildcard-source collision never occurs. The socket's source IP will match your
+`ip rule from` clauses:
+
+```toml
+[[paths]]
+name = "starlink"
+source_addr = "192.168.1.10"
+bind = "source"
+
+[[paths]]
+name = "5g"
+source_addr = "192.168.2.10"
+bind = "source"
+```
+
+Accepted `bind` values (per-path, optional) are:
+
+- `"source"` — force source-IP binding (defeats device-bind roam tolerance, safe for
+  policy-routed topologies)
+- `"device"` — force device binding (requires manual rule workaround for policy routing)
+- `"auto"` (default) — automatic heuristic (device bind on one-address interfaces, source
+  bind on multi-address)
+
+Restart the daemon after changing the config:
+
+```sh
+systemctl restart wanbond-edge
+```
+
 ## 4. systemd units
 
 Unit files live in `packaging/systemd/`:

content.text: {"item":{"id":"D38","milestoneId":"M28","status":"open","fields":{"headline":"Auto device-bind silently defeats `ip rule from <source_addr>` policy routing on single-address VLAN-per-WAN edges","severity":"high","description":"[fixes-doc D4, S1 — production deploy; workaround applied on the Pi.] selectDeviceBinds (internal/bind/pathsock.go) picks SO_BINDTODEVICE (wildcard source) for a one-address/one-path interface — EXACTLY the VLAN-per-WAN edge topology (eth0.231/eth0.232, one address each, per-WAN pinning via `ip rule from <source_addr>`). A wildcard-source socket never matches `ip rule from <source_addr>`, so the route lookup falls through to `main` (which has no route to the concentrator via that VLAN) → ENETUNREACH → silent path-down, ZERO packets on the wire, while `ping -I <source_ip>` proves the WAN works. Worked around in production with `ip rule add oif <dev> table N`. The T16/R23 conditional device-bind gating (single-address + single-path interface) was designed for roam survival but is precisely the wrong choice under source-based policy routing — the two selection criteria collide. Related: defects:D30 (runtime-added paths never device-bind — the mirror-image gap).","suggestedFix":"Two parts: (1) a config toggle to force source-IP binding per path or globally (`bind = \"source\"`) so source-policy-routing topologies can opt out of device-bind (fixes-doc I5; roam survival is moot there); consider making that mode the DEFAULT when the config implies source-routed WANs, or detecting the `from <source>` rule. (2) Document the collision + the `ip rule add oif <dev> table N` recipe until then (fixes-doc C2). Decide in plan-flow whether detection is feasible or the toggle suffices.","sourceRefs":["wanbond-fixes.md §A D4","wanbond-fixes.md §B I5","wanbond-fixes.md §C C2"],"ledgerRefs":["defects:D30","tasks:T16"],"tags":["production-deploy","device-bind","policy-routing"]},"createdAt":"2026-07-13T22:48:59.152Z","updatedAt":"2026-07-13T22:48:59.152Z","author":"fable-5","session":"cac93b81-5292-42e3-b77e-962544c75e54"}}

24-A change that moves the code but not the docs it invalidates is **incomplete**.

30:## Build, test, lint
31-
32:The dev shell (`nix develop`) puts Go 1.26, golangci-lint, and the netem/DPI
33-tooling on `PATH`. Run tools from it (e.g. `nix develop --command bash -c '…'`).
34-
35-```sh
36-just build      # go build ./...
37-just test       # unprivileged unit/property tests (go test ./...)
38:just lint       # go vet + golangci-lint, INCLUDING -tags e2e and -tags realhosts
39-just e2e        # privileged netns fixture: sudo -E go test -tags e2e ./test/e2e/...
40-just realhosts  # opt-in real-machine tier (-tags realhosts), report-only
41-```
42-
43:The non-privileged gate a change must pass before merge:
44-
45-```sh
46-go build ./... && go vet ./... && test -z "$(gofmt -l cmd internal test)" && go test ./...
47-```
48-
--
50:default gate; validate them separately (see Testing discipline).

52-## Load-bearing invariants — do not break
53-
54-Full detail in [docs/design.md §Load-bearing invariants](docs/design.md). In
55-short:
--
83:  `goleak`/`NumGoroutine` gate, not a bare `-count` run).
84-- **The netns fixture is CPU/PPS-bound.** It validates *functional* bonding,
85-  failover, FEC recovery, and DPI — **not** absolute throughput or bufferbloat.
86-  Do not assert link-throughput numbers from it; use counter ratios / functional
87:  checks. Real throughput belongs to the real-host tier / manual checklist.
88-- **Hardware-validate** changes that touch the netns fixture or real-host
89-  behavior on the standing worker machines (an amd64 NAT edge + an aarch64 public
90-  concentrator) before considering them done; these tiers are report-only and not
91:  in the default gate.
92-- Never commit code changes on a ledger/docs commit and vice versa; keep commits
93-  scoped.
94-
95-## Conventions
96-

All evidence gathered. Ledger-ID references are established install.md style, so that's fine. The gate is green; the disqualifying finding is the workaround recipe itself.

### Session summary
- **Did:** reviewed task T112 round 1 (diff 52792c5..509bc5b, docs/install.md +64 lines) — grounded the `bind` toggle against `internal/config/config.go` (per-path L488-494, top-level L50-54, values L84-92) and the D38 mechanism against `internal/bind/pathsock.go` `selectDeviceBinds` (L76-143); compared the recipe against the D38 defect record; ran the full non-privileged gate.
- **Achieved:** verdict disapprove; 5 criticisms / 0 questions.
- **Discovered:** gate fully green (`go build && go vet && gofmt && go test ./...` exit 0, internal/bind ok — no flake observed). Symptom, root-cause mechanism, `bind` field shape/values, `pre-T16` attribution, and `wanbond-edge` unit name all match source. FAILED acceptance: the recipe is not "verbatim from the production deploy" — D38 records `ip rule add oif <dev> table N`, but the doc ships `ip rule add from <source_ip> oif <dev> table <N>`; fib-rule selectors AND together and the device-bound socket presents a wildcard source at route-lookup time (the doc's own root-cause paragraph), so the `from` selector prevents the rule from ever matching — the workaround as written does not work.
- **Issues:** none; codegraph index was from the main worktree, so all load-bearing claims were re-verified against worktree files directly.

  "taskId": "T112",
  "verdict": "disapprove",
  "criticism": [
    "Workaround 1 recipe is self-defeating and deviates from the production-validated recipe the acceptance requires verbatim: the doc ships `ip rule add from <source_ip> oif <dev> table <N> prio 100`, but fib-rule selectors AND together and the device-bound (SO_BINDTODEVICE, wildcard-source) socket presents an unset source at route-lookup time — exactly the doc's own root-cause claim ('a wildcard-source socket never matches ip rule from') — so the `from <source_ip>` selector guarantees the rule never matches the tunnel's flow. D38 records the Pi workaround as `ip rule add oif <dev> table N` with NO from-clause. Drop the `from` selector from the workaround rule.",
    "Ungrounded property claim: 'This rule is persistent across policy-route changes and survives ip rule re-ordering' — nothing makes this rule persistent, and like every `ip rule` it does not survive reboot. Replace with an accurate caveat (not reboot-persistent; persist via the operator's existing mechanism, cf. the §5 netfilter-persistence pattern / addressing oneshot).",
    "Section numbering out of order: new '### 3b' is inserted at line 740, AFTER '### 3z. Full configuration reference' (line 499), while 3a sits at line 319 — reading order is 3a → 3z → 3b. Move the section between 3a and 3z, or renumber.",
    "Minor accuracy: the 'auto' bullet ('device bind on one-address interfaces, source bind on multi-address') omits the third condition — selectDeviceBinds (internal/bind/pathsock.go L137) also requires exactly ONE configured path resolving to that interface; two paths on one device fall back to source binding. One clause fixes it.",
    "Minor completeness: the doc presents `bind` as per-path only, but config also ships a top-level default `bind` (internal/config/config.go L50-54) applied to every path that omits its own — for the VLAN-per-WAN topology where every path needs source mode, a single top-level `bind = \"source\"` is the simpler recommendation; at minimum mention it exists."
